# PR Quality Gate — Pattern Catalog

Empirical catalog of patterns the GitHub Copilot PR reviewer flags across consuming projects. Each entry is a single markdown table row + JSON `params` per the schema in `README.md` §"Catalog grammar".

This catalog is **project-deidentified**: signatures and patterns are abstract; consuming projects do not need to customize it. FP entries are inline (no separate file).

<!-- Schema reminder (per README.md §Catalog grammar):
     | slug | scope_mode | params | review_pass_only_prompt | fp_slug | tier |
     Pipe characters inside JSON values escaped as \|. Parser unescapes before JSON.parse.
     Tier (6th column) added on lightweight-gate-v5. Values: HIGH | MEDIUM | LOW.
     Legacy 5-cell rows default to tier=MEDIUM with a stderr diagnostic; sunset by next catalog edit. -->

## Tier semantics (added with 6-cell schema bump)

Tier controls cost optimization in lighter panel modes AND `HIGH-TIER-SLUGS.md` generation:

- **HIGH** (rg-rules + review-pass-only): rg-rules run in every mode (full/triage/lint-only); review-pass-only emit `unreviewed_review_pass_only_slugs` warning in lint-only.
- **MEDIUM** (rg-rules + review-pass-only): run in full/triage only; SKIP in lint-only.
- **LOW** (rg-rules + review-pass-only): run in full only; SKIP in triage and lint-only.

Tier auto-derivation by `scripts/sync-critical-rules.ps1`:
- rg-detectable rules (`scope_mode: diff-scoped|tree-scoped|hybrid`): tier from rg-corpus hit count
- review-pass-only rules: tier from `panel-misses.csv` hit count (unique `pr_ref` count, not raw row count)
- Thresholds: ≥5 hits = HIGH, 3-4 = MEDIUM, 0-2 = LOW
- Manual override: row's `tier` column set to explicit value overrides auto-derivation

## Patterns (high-frequency battery, ≥5 hits in seed corpus)

| slug | scope_mode | params | review_pass_only_prompt | fp_slug | tier |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| doc-impl-mismatch | review-pass-only | {} | For every modified source file with XML docs, inline comments, PR description text, or README claims: read each prose claim and verify the surrounding code actually does what the prose says. Common failure modes: XML doc claims thread-context that captured SC contradicts; PR description names a feature the markup does not render; `<see cref>` references that won't resolve; "this delegates to X via COM" when X uses procedural API. **Interface-extension sub-case** (frequently missed; consuming-pr-8 round 3): when an interface or class gets NEW members in the diff (`HasActiveLogs` + `ReloadAllActiveLogsAsync` added to an interface whose summary previously described only the older concern), verify the interface-level `<summary>` enumerates / describes the new members or at least acknowledges the new responsibility surface. A summary that still describes only the pre-extension subset is `doc-impl-mismatch`. **DI-registration-method contract sub-case** (frequently missed; consuming-pr-8 round 7): when a DI registration method (`AddXyz` extension method on `IServiceCollection`, equivalent on `IHostBuilder`, `IApplicationBuilder.UseXyz`, ASP.NET Core `services.AddOptions<T>()`, Autofac `ContainerBuilder.RegisterXyz`, etc.) has an XML summary that documents required external registrations / host-provided dependencies (typical "Callers MUST also register: AddFluxor, AddXxx, ..." pattern), verify that EVERY new singleton/scoped/transient registration in the diff which depends on a host-provided abstraction is reflected in the contract doc. Adding a new registration that depends on `IHostProvidedService` without updating the "callers must register" list means a downstream host calling the registration method gets a DI activation failure at runtime instead of a compile-time / config-time signal. Flag every prose-vs-code divergence. |  | HIGH |
| comment-necessity | review-pass-only | {} | For every comment ADDED in the diff (`//` inline, `/* */` block, `///` XML doc — NOT pre-existing comments in the baseline that happen to live in modified files): verify it clarifies genuinely subtle behavior. **BEFORE adding any comment, evaluate code-clarity refactors first**: would renaming a variable / method / parameter, extracting a helper, or restructuring a conditional eliminate the need for the comment? If yes, refactor instead of commenting. Only comment if the refactor would make the code LESS clear (e.g., name would be too long, extraction would hurt locality). Flag comments that (a) restate what the code obviously does (e.g., `// Increment counter` above `counter++;`), (b) reference panel artifacts (slot numbers, round numbers, PR-bundle labels like `PR 1+2`, panel-phase names, "the bot caught this"), (c) duplicate XML method summaries with inline comments, (d) include multi-line XML `<remarks>` blocks on internal types, (e) narrate refactor/PR history inline, (f) explain a contract that the method/test NAME already conveys (e.g., `// Lock-release invariant` comment above a test named `LockReleasedBetweenCallsAndBothComplete` — name carries intent, comment redundant). Acceptable: brief single-line clarifications for non-obvious *why* (concurrency invariants the code cannot self-document, race-safety rationale, BCL quirks with version citation, FP override citations, spec/standard references); brief `///<summary>` on public APIs. Apply the principle: "Only comment code that needs a bit of clarification. Do not comment otherwise." Use the diff to distinguish NEW comments from baseline. |  | HIGH |
| strongly-typed-id-preference | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW declaration in the diff that introduces a primitive (`Guid`, `long`, `int`, `string`, `uint`, `ulong`, `short`, `ushort`) as an ID surface, in ANY of these positions: (i) field/property/parameter/local whose name ends with `Id`/`ID`/`id`; (ii) generic type argument that semantically represents an ID (e.g., `Dictionary<long, ModalSession>` where the `long` key is a modal ID; `HashSet<Guid>` of filter IDs; `IDictionary<TKey, TValue>` instantiations where `TKey` is a domain identifier): check whether a strongly-typed ID wrapper (`public readonly record struct XxxId(Guid Value)` or equivalent) already exists in the codebase for this domain. If a wrapper exists, USE IT. If no wrapper exists and this introduces a NEW logical ID domain, prefer creating one following the established codebase pattern (factory `public static XxxId Create() => new(Guid.NewGuid());`). **Acceptable primitives (do NOT flag)**: extensions of an existing primitive-ID subsystem where the surrounding public surface already uses primitives AND switching one site would create inconsistency (the reviewer MUST cite the specific pre-existing primitive ID surface to invoke this carve-out — dodge-by-assertion is not sufficient); EF Core entity row IDs that match the database schema (`long Id`, `int Id`); framework-required types (Blazor `[Parameter] public string Id { get; set; }`, `[Parameter] public Guid SelectorId { get; set; }`); Windows platform IDs (`ushort Id`, `int Id` per platform conventions); interop / P/Invoke handles; correlation/trace/request/diagnostic tokens scoped to single-operation tracing, where the value never crosses domain boundaries. Use the diff to distinguish NEW IDs from extensions of existing primitive-ID code; baseline `long Id` properties on entities are NOT in scope. |  |
| prefer-async-suffix | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW sync call added in the diff INSIDE a genuinely async method — defined as: the enclosing method declaration has the `async` keyword OR the method body contains `await` somewhere (this excludes sync bridge/adapter methods that merely return `Task`/`Task<T>`/`ValueTask`/`ValueTask<T>` via `Task.CompletedTask`, `Task.FromResult`, or by returning the result of another sync call): verify whether the receiver type has a `<Method>Async` sibling. If yes, prefer the async overload. **If choosing sync where Async exists**, the call MUST be commented with the reason (e.g., `// sync bridge: awaiting captures SynchronizationContext`, `// sync OK: caller holds non-async lock`). Unjustified sync-when-Async-exists is a flag-able violation regardless of the rest of the diff. **High-signal sites to check**: `DbConnection.Open()` / `SqliteConnection.Open()` / `SqlConnection.Open()` → `OpenAsync()` (note: `IDbConnection` itself does NOT define `OpenAsync` — only the concrete/abstract `DbConnection` hierarchy does); `DbContext.SaveChanges()` → `SaveChangesAsync()` (also caught by `async-correctness` rg — DO NOT duplicate-flag if `async-correctness` already surfaced it); `Stream.Dispose()` → `await DisposeAsync()`; `Stream.Read/Write/Flush()` → `Async` versions; `CancellationTokenRegistration.Dispose()` → `DisposeAsync()`; `HttpClient.Send(HttpRequestMessage)` → `SendAsync()` (note: most `HttpClient.*` methods are already `Async`-only — only `Send` is the sync one); `IServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<IDbContextFactory<T>>().CreateDbContext()` → `CreateDbContextAsync()`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: types whose API has no `Async` sibling (`Utf8JsonReader.Read`, `Process.Start`, `ILogger.Debug`, `Math.Sqrt`); sync calls in non-async contexts (`Dispose()` on a `using` statement at end of a sync method — `using` itself handles the dispose form); sync bridge/adapter implementations of an async-shaped contract (e.g., `public Task SaveAsync() { _writer.Flush(); return Task.CompletedTask; }`); explicit diagnostic-only synchronous I/O where the cost is intentional and commented; sites already surfaced by `async-correctness` rg battery — do NOT duplicate-flag. Use the diff to find NEW sync calls in async contexts; do NOT flag pre-existing sync calls in unchanged code. |  | HIGH |
| publication-barrier-order | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified writer method in the diff that updates multiple fields under a single lock AND publishes one of them via `Volatile.Write` / `Interlocked.Exchange` / `Interlocked.CompareExchange` as a "publication barrier" intended to make the rest visible to cross-thread readers: verify the volatile/interlocked write happens LAST in the write sequence (so it acts as the publication point), NOT first or in the middle. The .NET memory model guarantees writes preceding a `Volatile.Write` (or full-fence `Interlocked` op) are not reordered past it — so any cross-thread reader that observes the volatile-published value is guaranteed to also see the prior writes. If the volatile-write is first/middle, plain-field writes can be reordered past it from a CROSS-THREAD reader's perspective (the lock body's release barrier only fires on `Monitor.Exit`, but cross-thread readers using a DIFFERENT lock or no lock can observe the partial state during the writer's lock body). **High-signal patterns to check**: `Volatile.Write(ref _activeId, newId); ActiveType = newType;` (id published BEFORE state) — wrong; should be `ActiveType = newType; Volatile.Write(ref _activeId, newId);` (id LAST). Mirror for clear paths: clear plain fields first, then volatile-write sentinel id LAST. Also flag `_idCounter++; Volatile.Write(ref _activeId, _idCounter);` followed by plain writes — same issue, reorder so id-write is last. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: lock-protected reads via the SAME lock as the writer (mutual exclusion makes ordering irrelevant); writer-side-only state with no cross-thread readers; explicit `Interlocked.MemoryBarrier()` / `Thread.MemoryBarrier()` between writes (rare, only when the volatile-write semantics aren't sufficient). Apply to writer paths only; reader-side double-read patterns (re-read the volatile after plain reads) are a complementary concern, not this rule. |  |
| event-ordering-test-subscription | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW test in the diff that asserts ORDERING between an EVENT (e.g., `StateChanged?.Invoke()`, `PropertyChanged`, `INotifyPropertyChanged.PropertyChanged`) and a SUBJECT-ACTION (e.g., a TCS completion that resumes an awaiter, a state transition observable by the consumer): verify the test's event subscription happens AFTER any non-asserted setup action that fires the same event. Subscribing BEFORE a setup `Show()`/`Initialize()`/`Open()`/`Start()` that ALSO fires the event causes the SETUP event to set the assertion flag, masking whether the SUBJECT-ACTION-fired event actually preceded the awaiter. The test then passes regardless of correctness — a false-positive risk that defeats the test's purpose. **High-signal pattern**: ``sut.StateChanged += () => { if (!awaiterResumed) flag = true; }; sut.Show<...>(); ... sut.CancelActive();`` — the `Show` event sets `flag=true` before `CancelActive` runs. **Fix options**: (a) move `+= ...` AFTER the setup `Show()`, (b) count event invocations and assert on the Nth, (c) reset the flag between setup and subject-action. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: tests where the event is wired up via DI/constructor and there's no separable setup-vs-subject phase; tests asserting "event fires at least once" rather than "event fires before X"; tests where the setup-fired event and the subject-fired event are distinguishable by some other property (e.g., the event argument carries identity). Use the diff to find NEW tests; pre-existing test patterns with the same shape are not in scope. |  | HIGH |
| event-completion-fault-isolation | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified writer method in the diff that BOTH (a) invokes a public event (e.g., `StateChanged?.Invoke()`, `PropertyChanged?.Invoke(...)`, custom multi-cast delegate) OR a caller-supplied callback (`Action`, `Func<T>`, `Action<T>`, custom delegate parameter) AND (b) subsequently completes a `TaskCompletionSource`, invokes a captured cancel delegate, or otherwise resumes an awaiting caller: verify the post-event completion is wrapped in `try/finally` so the completion runs even when the subscriber/callback throws. Pattern that BREAKS: ``StateChanged?.Invoke(); tcs.TrySetResult(value);`` — if any subscriber throws, the TCS never completes and the awaiter hangs forever. Same hang risk with caller callbacks: ``onComplete?.Invoke(); tcs.TrySetResult(value);``. Pattern that WORKS: ``try { StateChanged?.Invoke(); } finally { tcs.TrySetResult(value); }`` — TCS completion is guaranteed regardless of subscriber exceptions; the exception still propagates to the caller for diagnostics. This applies to ALL completion shapes: `TrySetResult`, `TrySetCanceled`, `TrySetException`, `cancelDelegate?.Invoke()` capturing a TCS, `IPromise.Resolve`, etc. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: event/callback invocations with no follow-up completion (purely notification); event invocations where the completion semantically depends on subscriber outcome (rare; document if so); event/callback invocations that follow rather than precede completion (e.g., `tcs.TrySetResult(value); StateChanged?.Invoke();` — but verify this ordering is intentional, since it can violate the publication-before-resume discipline that subscribers often rely on). Use the diff to find NEW writer paths; pre-existing event-then-complete patterns in unchanged code are not in scope. |  | HIGH |
| test-aaa-pattern | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW test method (`[Fact]` or `[Theory]`) added in the diff: verify the test body is structured with `// Arrange`, `// Act`, and `// Assert` markers separating the three phases (the AAA pattern). This convention is enforced across the codebase's existing test files and improves readability + reviewability. **Apply when**: a test has a discernible setup → action → verification flow. The markers must be on their own lines as comments (e.g., `        // Arrange`); inline comments don't count. **Acceptable variations (do NOT flag)**: tests with no Act phase (pure state assertions on initial construction) may use `// Arrange` + `// Assert` only — drop `// Act` rather than insert a placeholder; tests that mix Act+Assert (e.g., `Assert.Throws<T>(() => sut.Method())`) may combine via a single `// Act + Assert` marker; data-only `[Theory]` rows that exercise multiple cases in a loop may use the markers around the loop, not within. **NOT flagged**: pre-existing tests in modified files that lacked AAA before this PR (not in scope — AAA-converting baseline tests is separate cleanup work); micro-benchmarks; helper test methods that aren't `[Fact]`/`[Theory]`; generated/source-generated test files (files with `<auto-generated>` headers, files inside `obj/` or generator output directories, or files where the generator owns the layout — adding AAA markers to generator output is not a valid remediation). Use the diff to find NEW hand-authored tests; mirror the existing codebase convention. |  |
| panel-artifact-leakage | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"\\b(Slot \\d+\|R\\d+ (finding\|fix\|round\|rework\|ready)\|PR \\d+\\+\\d+\|pre-(implementation\|PR-creation) panel\|round[ -]?\\d+\|bot.*(caught\|finding\|flagged))\\b","glob":["*.cs"]} |  |  | HIGH |
| resource-cleanup | hybrid | {"tree":{"pattern":"new\\s+(CancellationTokenSource\|SqliteConnection\|SqlConnection\|FileStream\|StreamReader\|StreamWriter\|HttpClient\|ProcessStartInfo)\\(","glob":["*.cs"]},"diff":{"pattern":"new\\s+(CancellationTokenSource\|SqliteConnection\|SqlConnection\|FileStream\|StreamReader\|StreamWriter\|HttpClient\|ProcessStartInfo)\\(","glob":["*.cs"]}} |  |  |
| naming-convention | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"^\\s+var [A-Z][a-z]","glob":["tests/**/*.cs"]} |  |  |
| async-correctness | hybrid | {"tree":{"pattern":"SaveChanges\\(\\)\|_ = InvokeAsync\|\\.Result\\b\|\\.Wait\\(\\)","glob":["*.cs"]},"diff":{"pattern":"SaveChanges\\(\\)\|_ = InvokeAsync\|\\.Result\\b\|\\.Wait\\(\\)","glob":["*.cs"]}} |  |  |
| thread-safety | tree-scoped | {"pattern":"IProgress<.*>\|new Progress<","glob":["*.cs"]} |  |  |
| bounds-empty-collection | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"\\b\\w+\\[0\\]\|stackalloc \\w+\\[\\w+\\.Length\\]","glob":["*.cs"]} |  |  |
| aria-binding | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"aria-\|_is[A-Z]\\w+\\s*=\\s*(true\|false\|!_)","glob":["*.razor","*.razor.cs"]} |  |  |
| regex-validation | tree-scoped | {"pattern":"new Regex\\(\|filter.*regex\|regex.*filter","glob":["*.cs","*.razor"]} |  | fp-1 |
| razor-binding | hybrid | {"tree":{"pattern":":empty","glob":["*.css"]},"diff":{"pattern":"@onscroll\|@onkeydown\|@onkeyup\|(public\|protected) (async )?(void\|Task) (Show\|Hide\|Toggle\|Open\|Close)\\w*","glob":["*.razor","*.razor.cs"]}} |  |  |
| null-handling | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"\"\\[.*\\{[^}]*\\}\\]\"","glob":["*.cs"]} |  |  |
| dead-code-unused | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified `.cs` / `.ts` / `.tsx` / `.js` / `.jsx` file in the diff: identify symbols introduced by the PR that have no incoming reference. Common cases: a private method called by only one site that the same PR removes (the method is now dead); a parameter added to a method that the body never reads; a local variable assigned but never observed; an enum value with no producer or consumer; an import/using statement added for a type the PR no longer references; a removed `if` branch that leaves a captured local unused. Flag the dead symbol and propose deletion. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: public API surface (callers may live in other repos / out-of-tree consumers); test-only helpers added in the same PR that the test file itself uses; symbols guarded behind `[Conditional("DEBUG")]` / `#if` directives; designer-generated members; framework conventions that look unused but are reflected over (e.g., Blazor `[Parameter]`, Fluxor `[FeatureState]`, EF Core navigation properties, `[JsonPropertyName]`-decorated DTOs, MSTest/xUnit attribute-discovered methods). Use the diff to find the NEW symbols; do not regress on legacy unused code that predates this PR. |  |
| null-dereference-risk | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW receiver-access (`obj.Member`, `obj[index]`, `obj()`, `obj!.Member`, optional chaining `obj?.Member.X` where `.X` is non-null-safe) added in the diff: verify the receiver cannot be `null` at that program point. Specifically: track null-conditional propagation (`a?.B.C` — if `a` is null then `.B` is null then `.C` NREs), parameter origins for nullable-annotated parameters consumed unguarded, fields declared without initializer that can race null-default with init, return-value chains where an upstream method can return null. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: receivers already guarded by `if (x is null) return` / `ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(x)` / `Debug.Assert(x is not null)` immediately above; non-nullable annotated locals/parameters with no flow that could produce null (full compiler NRT analysis suffices — do not double-flag what the compiler already enforces); `!` null-forgiving operator on a receiver where the surrounding code has just type-tested or pattern-matched. Use the diff to identify NEW receiver accesses; pre-existing potential-null patterns in unchanged code are not in scope. |  |
| code-comment-mismatch | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW inline comment (`//`) or block comment (`/* */`) on a NEW or MODIFIED line in the diff: verify the comment text matches what the adjacent code now does. Distinct from `doc-impl-mismatch` (which covers prose claims in XML docs / PR description / README): this rule targets the COMMENT-TO-CODE drift where a prior comment was preserved through a refactor that changed the surrounding logic. **High-signal patterns**: `// Create TestButton` above `var x = new Label("TestLabel");` (creates Label, not Button); `// Deadlock on iOS .NET 10` above code that does not exhibit that deadlock; `// Cancel and dispose` above a method body that no longer disposes; `// Returns null when X` above a method whose new body always returns a default; `// 5 seconds` above `TimeSpan.FromMilliseconds(3000)`. **Extraction-drift sub-case** (frequently missed; consuming-pr-8 round 4): when a helper method (or interface, or class) is EXTRACTED from a larger method in the diff, comments that traveled with the extracted code often describe operations the new location does NOT perform — e.g., a catch-block comment saying "torn down between the _disposed check and the dispatcher call" preserved into an extracted helper whose body has only the dispatcher call (no preceding _disposed check). For every NEW method body whose code matches a pattern that previously lived in a larger method (extraction signature: new private/internal method whose body is a shorter recombination of code from another method in the diff), audit each comment for references to variables, local guards, or sibling operations that aren't present in the extracted scope. Flag every drift. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: comments that describe *general intent* and remain accurate even if implementation detail changes (e.g., `// Compute the running total` above any aggregate); comments explaining a non-obvious *why* (concurrency invariant, race-safety) that the code still upholds; comments referencing a spec/standard that the code conforms to. Use the diff to identify comments whose adjacent code changed; pre-existing comments on baseline-unmodified lines are not in scope. |  | HIGH |
| behavior-suppressed-by-guard | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW early-return / `if (...) return;` / `if (...) return null;` / `if (...) throw;` / `if (...) { ... return; }` guard added at the top of a public or virtual method body: verify the guard does not silently suppress a code path the prior implementation supported. **High-signal patterns**: `if (Window is null) return;` added to a method that previously queued the request and replayed it after `Window` was assigned (the early-return now drops legitimate queued calls); `if (!_initialized) return;` added to a method whose callers depend on it executing even pre-init; `if (cancellationToken.IsCancellationRequested) return;` at the entry of a finalizer/cleanup method (cleanup must run regardless of cancellation). Flag the guard and propose: (a) inversion to a precondition exception, (b) deferred queueing instead of dropping, or (c) explicit no-op-with-trace if silent-drop is intended. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: guards added with explicit log/telemetry surfacing the early-return; guards whose dropped path is provably equivalent to the post-guard path (e.g., `if (list.Count == 0) return;` before a foreach that would no-op anyway); ArgumentException-throwing guards on invalid input. Use the diff to find NEW guards; pre-existing guards that simply moved around are not in scope. |  |
| file-path-injection-traversal | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `Path.Combine` / `Path.GetFullPath` / `File.*` / `Directory.*` / `FileStream(...)` / `new StreamReader(path)` / shell-out / `string.Format("...{0}", path)` call added in the diff whose path argument derives from external input (`HttpRequest`, query string, posted form, JSON body, environment variable, command-line argument, file upload, websocket message, `[Parameter]` from URL): verify the path is canonicalized AND validated against a `..` traversal AND constrained to an allow-list of base directories. **High-signal failure**: `var p = Path.Combine(baseDir, userInput); File.ReadAllText(p);` — `userInput = "../../etc/passwd"` escapes `baseDir`. Required defense: compute `Path.GetFullPath(p)` and assert it starts-with the canonicalized base. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: path arguments that are static / compile-time constants; paths from a configuration value the operator owns (still flag if the config source is user-writable); paths under a per-user scratch directory where traversal can only escape into another user-writable area; OS-level chroot/sandbox enforced before the path is consumed (cite the sandbox). Use the diff to find NEW path-consuming sites with external-input lineage; existing already-validated paths are not in scope. |  |
| config-default-missing | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `Configuration[key]` / `IConfiguration.GetValue<T>(key)` / `Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable(key)` / `_config.GetSection(key)` / `process.env.KEY` / `ConfigurationManager.AppSettings[key]` read added in the diff: verify the consuming code documents and handles the missing-key case. **Required**: either (a) `?? defaultValue` on the read with the default named in source or a doc comment, (b) `?? throw new InvalidOperationException("ConfigKey \"X\" is required")` if it must be set, or (c) an explicit upstream presence-check in the same PR (e.g., a startup validator that fails fast). **High-signal failure**: `var connStr = _config["ConnectionStrings:Db"]; new SqlConnection(connStr);` — passes `null` to the connection constructor and throws NRE far from the misconfiguration; reviewer cannot trace cause. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: configuration consumed via Options pattern bound at startup where the binder already throws on missing required properties (`[Required]` data annotation, `services.AddOptions<T>().ValidateDataAnnotations()`); test-only configuration with hardcoded fallbacks; configuration keys whose absence is semantically valid (feature-flag opt-in). Use the diff to find NEW config reads; pre-existing patterns are not in scope. |  |
| typo-naming-inconsistency | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW identifier (type name, member name, parameter, local, file name, route segment, JSON property name, GitHub Actions step id, secret key) added in the diff: scan the surrounding file (within 200 lines or the same class/module) for adjacent identifiers that follow a consistent stem. Flag identifiers that break the stem: `OnFooChanged` adjacent to `OnBarChange` (Changed vs Change); `databaseId` parameter where the surrounding API uses `dbId`; `[Parameter] public string OperationId` where the rest of the component uses `RequestId`; CSS class `.btn-primary` adjacent to `.button-secondary`; route `/api/customer` adjacent to `/api/customers`. Also flag plain typos: `Recieve`, `Calender`, `Lable`, `Seperate`, `Occured`, double-letter mistakes (`acommodate`, `addres`). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: language-required spellings (color vs colour — match the surrounding repo convention; `Color` is canonical .NET); intentional version-bump renames (`V2`, `New`, `Legacy` suffixes); identifiers documented in an external API contract whose spelling we cannot change. Use the diff to find NEW identifiers; pre-existing naming drift in baseline code is not in scope. |  |
| attribute-or-decorator-missing | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW class / method / property / parameter added in the diff that participates in a framework convention requiring a marker attribute: verify the attribute is present. **High-signal cases**: ASP.NET Core controller action method without `[HttpGet]`/`[HttpPost]`/etc. when the routing requires explicit verbs; Blazor `[Parameter]` missing on a public property a parent component is supposed to bind; EF Core model property whose column requires `[Required]` / `[MaxLength]` / `[Column(TypeName = "...")]` to match the schema; xUnit `[Theory]` without `[InlineData]` / `[MemberData]` / `[ClassData]`; `[Authorize]` missing on a controller action that handles sensitive data when the rest of the controller is authorized; Fluxor `[FeatureState]` / `[ReducerMethod]` missing on state classes / reducer methods; serialization attributes (`[JsonPropertyName]`, `[XmlElement]`) missing where the surrounding API uses them consistently. Flag the missing attribute and cite the framework convention. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: convention-by-name routing (ASP.NET Core attribute-less methods that follow `[controller]/[action]` patterns where the surrounding controller does the same); test-only helper classes that don\u0027t participate in the framework; non-public members not exposed to the framework. Use the diff to find NEW framework-participating declarations. |  |
| localization-i18n-gap | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW user-visible string added in the diff — defined as: a `string` literal passed to a UI-rendering API (Blazor `@text`, MAUI `<Label Text="...">`, MAUI `DisplayAlert`, `IAlertDialogService.ShowAlert`, `Console.WriteLine` in CLI tools meant for end-users, exception messages thrown to user-facing layers, `TraceLogger.Information` if log surfaces to UI), OR a string in a `.razor` markup line that renders directly: verify the string goes through the project\u0027s localization mechanism (`.resx`, `IStringLocalizer<T>`, `@inject IStringLocalizer<T>`, custom resource catalog) — not hard-coded. **High-signal failure**: `<Label Text="An operation is running. Cancel and close anyway?">` — non-English users see English. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: debug log strings (`TraceLogger.Debug`) that surface only to developers; exception messages whose audience is developers reading stack traces; system-language-locked strings (date formats handled by `CultureInfo`); strings that ARE the canonical name of a concept (`"SettingsModal"` as a key, `"db"` as a tab id) — these are identifiers, not user-visible text; placeholders/test-data in tests. Use the diff to find NEW UI-rendered strings; pre-existing non-localized strings in baseline are not in scope (separate localization sweep). |  |
| race-condition-thread-safety | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW shared mutable state added in the diff (instance field, static field, class-level collection) that is read OR written from MORE THAN ONE thread / async continuation / callback: verify access is synchronized. Concurrency surfaces: ASP.NET Core request handlers (one instance per request but singleton dependencies share state); Blazor singleton services (StateChanged events fire across renders); event handlers + reducer state; cancellation callbacks; `IProgress<T>.Report` callbacks; timer ticks; HTTP client response handlers; thread-pool work items. **Required**: `lock`/`ReaderWriterLockSlim`/`SemaphoreSlim`/`Interlocked`/`Volatile`/immutable-replace pattern. Distinct from `publication-barrier-order` (which is about write-order within an already-locked path): this rule is about whether the lock exists at all. **High-signal failure**: `private readonly Dictionary<long, X> _items = new(); ... void OnEvent() { _items.Add(...); } void OnQuery() { return _items[key]; }` — concurrent read/write throws InvalidOperationException. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: immutable types (record, `ImmutableXxx`, `FrozenXxx`); state owned by a single confined thread/dispatcher with documented ownership; state behind an existing synchronization primitive the diff doesn\u0027t remove; thread-local storage. Use the diff to find NEW shared mutable state; pre-existing synchronization in baseline is not in scope. |  |
| naming-convention-violation | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW identifier declaration in the diff: verify the naming style matches the language/framework convention AND the codebase\u0027s existing convention. **High-signal patterns**: C# local variable with `_` prefix (`_` prefix is reserved for instance fields); C# private field WITHOUT `_` prefix when the codebase otherwise uses it; C# public member in `camelCase` instead of `PascalCase`; TypeScript class in `camelCase`; TypeScript interface with `I` prefix when the surrounding code uses no `I` prefix; method name starting with verb-vs-noun mismatch (`Computation` vs `Compute`); `Async` suffix on a non-async method; missing `Async` suffix on a method returning `Task`/`Task<T>` (related to `prefer-async-suffix` but for naming, not invocation). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: identifiers matching established cross-cutting convention even if it differs from the language default (e.g., `_camelCase` private fields when the rest of the file/repo uses that style — flag inconsistency, not the convention itself); identifiers framework-required to use a specific style (`__Builder` for MAUI), `loopback-` for routing). Use the diff to find NEW identifiers; pre-existing naming variance is not in scope. |  |
| github-actions-not-pinned-to-sha | hybrid | {"tree":{"pattern":"uses:\\s*[^@]+@v\\d","glob":[".github/workflows/*.yml",".github/workflows/*.yaml"]},"diff":{"pattern":"uses:\\s*[^@]+@v\\d","glob":[".github/workflows/*.yml",".github/workflows/*.yaml"]}} |  |  |
| silent-fallback-loses-info | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `??` / `?? default` / `try/catch { return null; }` / `try/catch { return ""; }` / `if (...) return defaultValue;` / `catch (Exception) { /* ignored */ }` added in the diff where the fallback path is reached on an actual failure (parse error, missing resource, IO exception, downstream service unavailable): verify the fallback either logs/traces/reports the cause OR raises an alarm OR the failure is genuinely non-actionable. **High-signal failure**: `try { return JsonSerializer.Deserialize<X>(json); } catch { return null; }` — caller sees null and assumes "not present" rather than "malformed input"; the malformed input never gets reported. Required: at minimum `_traceLogger.Warning($"Failed to deserialize X: {ex}")` before returning the fallback. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: fallbacks where the missing-data semantic is genuinely the same as the failure-data semantic (e.g., `int.TryParse(s, out var n) ? n : 0` where the caller treats both as "no count"); fallbacks documented at the API boundary as "returns default on any failure"; fallbacks where the upstream caller is expected to retry; fallbacks under a global exception handler that already logs everything. Use the diff to find NEW silent-fallback patterns; pre-existing silent fallbacks in baseline are not in scope. |  | HIGH |
| nullable-optional-callback-with-surprising-no-op-default | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW callback parameter (`Func<...>?`, `Action<...>?`, custom delegate) declared as nullable with a default value (`= null`) in a public/internal API: verify that the null-default behavior is either (a) the SAFEST option that PROCEEDS with the underlying operation in a documented way (e.g., `askOverwriteAsync = null → skip-conflicts → still import non-conflicting files`) OR (b) explicitly documented in the XML doc at the parameter level with a `<param>` tag describing the null-default. If null-default silently REFUSES the operation (e.g., `confirmRemoveAsync = null → return NotConfirmed → no removal`), that's a surprising API contract — callers will assume omission means "no callback needed, proceed" but actual behavior is "no callback means refuse". Flag the pattern. Fix options: (a) make the callback REQUIRED (drop `? = null` from the signature; add `ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull` at entry); (b) rename to make the no-op semantics explicit (`tryConfirmRemoveAsync`); (c) add an XML `<param>` doc explicitly documenting the null-default behavior. **Asymmetry red flag**: if sibling methods on the same interface follow DIFFERENT null-default conventions (Import callback null → proceed-with-skip; Remove callback null → refuse), the asymmetry MUST be documented at each parameter — otherwise the inconsistency surprises callers. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: callback parameters where null-default IS the safest behavior for the operation type (non-destructive operations like Import where null → skip is ergonomic AND documented); callbacks whose XML doc already explicitly states the null-default behavior; private/internal helpers where the call-site is controlled. Use the diff to identify NEW callback declarations. **Cited from consuming-pr-8 round 6** (panel-miss): `RemoveDatabaseAsync` had `Func<...>? confirmRemoveAsync = null` with null-default = silent refusal; flagged + fixed by making the callback required. |  | HIGH |
| output-format-incompatible-with-new-renderer | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified site in the diff that builds an output string (`StringBuilder.AppendLine` + bullet glyphs, `\n`-separated lines, tab-aligned columns, indented sub-items, ANSI color escapes, HTML entities, Markdown) AND routes that output to a DIFFERENT renderer than the original site (e.g., previously rendered via `AlertDialog` with `white-space: pre-wrap` CSS → now rendered via `Banner` with `white-space: nowrap` + `text-overflow: ellipsis`; previously to stdout terminal → now to JSON log line; previously to email body → now to push notification): verify the new renderer can preserve the formatting OR change the output format to match the new renderer's constraints. **High-signal failure** (cited from consuming-pr-8 round 8): `BuildImportSummary` emits `StringBuilder.AppendLine` + `\u2022` bullets for multi-failure detail; the original code routed to `IAlertDialogService.ShowAlert` whose CSS uses `white-space: pre-wrap` (newlines preserved). PR 8 re-routed to `IInfoBannerService.ReportInfoBanner` / `IErrorBannerService.ReportError` whose CSS (`banner.css:71-78`) uses `white-space: nowrap` + `text-overflow: ellipsis` — newlines collapse to spaces, bullet list flattens to unreadable "successful. Failed: • db1: bad • db2: bad". **Required action**: when refactoring an OutputContent → Renderer pairing, audit every multi-line/formatted output for the new renderer's whitespace and width constraints; either reformat to single-line (with separator characters like `; ` or `, `) OR update renderer CSS to preserve formatting (where it doesn't conflict with the renderer's intended single-line design). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: output strings that are intrinsically single-line (no multi-line builder usage); renderers that preserve `\n` natively (`<pre>`, `white-space: pre-wrap`); output that is consumed by a programmatic parser, not a human renderer. Use the diff to find sites where formatted-output builders meet a new render-target. |  | HIGH |
| callback-swallows-exception-the-caller-expects-to-propagate | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified callback IMPLEMENTATION (lambda / local function / method passed as `Func<...>` / `Action<...>` / custom delegate) in the diff whose body contains `catch (XException) { return defaultValue; }` / `catch (XException) { return; }` / any other domain-value conversion of an exception type: locate the CALLER's invocation site for that callback and inspect its surrounding try/catch handlers. If the caller explicitly rethrows that exception type (`try { await callback(...); } catch (XException) { throw; }`) OR the caller's outer handler depends on that exception propagating to set distinct state (abort the whole operation, return a sentinel fallback outcome, log distinctly, skip subsequent work), then the callback's swallow-and-convert silently BREAKS the caller's contract — the caller will see the domain value (e.g., `false=Skip`) and continue as if the operation succeeded with that result, when it should have aborted. **High-signal failure** (cited from consuming-pr-8 round 9): `DatabaseOperationCoordinator.ResolveImportConflictsAsync` does `try { overwrite = await askOverwriteAsync(...); } catch (OperationCanceledException) { throw; }` so OCE bubbles up through `RunOperationAsync<T>` which catches OCE and returns `ImportOutcome.None` (silent cancel). The UI implementation `SettingsModal.AskOverwriteAsync` had `catch (OperationCanceledException) { return false; }` — converting cancellation to Skip-this-file. Result: the conflict-resolution loop kept iterating and the import proceeded with skipped conflicts instead of aborting. **Required action**: in the callback body, REMOVE the catch for any exception type the caller is documented (by explicit rethrow OR by catch-and-distinct-handling at the outer scope) to propagate. Keep catches only for exception types the caller has NO special handling for (those are safe to convert to a domain value). **Common pairing**: a `catch (ObjectDisposedException) { return false; }` line is usually safe (teardown is local to the callback's instance); a `catch (OperationCanceledException) { return false; }` line is almost always WRONG when the caller's contract uses cancellation to abort. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: callbacks where the caller swallows the same exception type with the same domain-value conversion (callback is duplicating caller's behavior, harmless); callbacks whose XML doc explicitly documents the swallow contract (`<param>` doc says "implementations may swallow OCE"); top-level `async void` event handlers swallowing for framework stability — the "caller" is the framework, no domain contract to violate. **Audit method**: for every NEW callback declaration in the diff, view its IMPLEMENTATION body AND its INVOCATION site in the producer; cross-reference the catch types. Particularly important after extracting orchestration logic from a UI component to a Runtime coordinator (the UI may have inherited a swallow that was safe in monolithic code but breaks the new contract). |  | HIGH |
| dispatcher-fire-and-forget-where-completion-must-be-awaited | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified method whose name OR XML doc OR call-site context implies SEQUENTIAL DEPENDENCY between dispatched steps (verbs like "reload", "reset", "rotate", "close-and-reopen", "drain-and-refill", "swap", "save-then-publish", "shutdown-then-start", "migrate", anything that promises a temporally-ordered multi-step operation) BUT whose body uses an event bus / dispatcher / mediator / message queue / `_ = SomeAsyncCall()` to enqueue work without awaiting completion of the dispatched effect before proceeding to the dependent step: verify completion is awaited via a `TaskCompletionSource`, `ManualResetEventSlim`, `Channel<T>` response, request/response correlation id, or explicit `await effect()` call. **High-signal failure** (cited from consuming-pr-8 round 10): `DatabaseCoordinationEffects.ReloadAllActiveLogsAsync` (named "reload" — clear sequential implication of close-then-reopen) executed `_dispatcher.Dispatch(new CloseAllLogsAction()); foreach (var log in snapshot) { _eventLogCommands.OpenLog(...); } return Task.CompletedTask;`. The `CloseAllLogsAction` effect handler does heavyweight async work (`CancelAllLoads`, `_logWatcherService.RemoveAllAsync()`, cache clears) that runs concurrently with the immediately-dispatched `OpenLog` actions — `CancelAllLoads` could cancel the new loads we just kicked off; `RemoveAllAsync` could remove watchers we just registered. Sibling method `PrepareForDatabaseRemovalAsync` did it CORRECTLY: `AcquireCoordinatorLockAsync` + per-log `RegisterCloseCompletion(tcs)` + dispatch per-log `CloseLogAction` + `await tcs.Task.WaitAsync(timeout, ct)` per log, THEN proceed to next phase. Fix applied: replicated the per-log close-await pattern in `ReloadAllActiveLogsAsync`. **Required action**: when adding a method that orchestrates an event-bus-dispatched multi-step sequence, mirror the established TCS-await pattern (or introduce one if absent). Make the method genuinely `async Task` (not `Task` returning `Task.CompletedTask`) and document the sequential semantics explicitly in the XML summary. **Audit method**: search NEW code for `Dispatch(`, `Send(`, `Mediator.`, `Publish(`, `Enqueue(`, `Post(`, `_ = SomeAsyncMethod(` patterns. For each occurrence, check (a) the surrounding method's name + XML doc for sequence-implying verbs; (b) whether the dispatched action's effect handler does async work (look at `[EffectMethod]` / handler signature for `async Task`); (c) whether the next step in the calling method depends on the dispatched work's completion. If yes-yes-yes → flag. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: methods whose name/doc explicitly state "fire-and-forget" / "dispatch only" / "queue request"; dispatches followed only by independent work (no temporal dependency on the dispatched effect); dispatched actions whose effect is pure synchronous reducer mutation (no `async` in the effect body, no IO, no cancellation); dispatches at the END of a method where the caller is meant to enqueue without waiting. |  | HIGH |
| dispose-leak | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `IDisposable` / `IAsyncDisposable` instance created in the diff (`new SqliteConnection(...)`, `new FileStream(...)`, `new HttpClient(...)`, `new CancellationTokenSource(...)`, `new SemaphoreSlim(...)`, `Image.FromStream(...)`, `UIImage.LoadFromData(...)` on iOS, native handles via P/Invoke): verify the instance is disposed on ALL exit paths from its scope — `using`/`await using` declaration, explicit `try/finally` with `Dispose()` in finally, or assignment to a field that the owning type disposes in its own `Dispose`/`DisposeAsync`. **High-signal failures**: `var image = UIImage.LoadFromData(data); var resized = image.Resize(...); return resized;` — `image` is not disposed; `var conn = new SqliteConnection(connStr); conn.Open(); return conn;` — caller may forget. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: BCL types known NOT to require disposal (`Task` typically; lambdas — see `iasyncdisposable-fp`); instances captured by a long-lived ownership chain where the eventual owner disposes (`AddSingleton<T>` ownership transferred to DI container); singleton handles allocated once at startup (`HttpClient` recommended-singleton pattern). Cross-reference: existing `resource-cleanup` regex flags some `new XxxX(...)` cases at a coarser grain; this rule does the semantic disposed-on-all-paths verification. |  |
| async-blocking-on-captured-syncctx | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `.Result` / `.Wait()` / `.GetAwaiter().GetResult()` in the diff on a `Task` / `Task<T>` whose continuation may run on a captured synchronization context (UI thread, ASP.NET classic SC, MAUI dispatcher): risk of deadlock. The blocking call holds the SC thread; the awaited continuation needs the same SC to resume → deadlock. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: `.Result` in test contexts where there's no captured SC (xUnit test runners, console-app `Main` BEFORE .NET 7 async-Main); `Task.Run(async () => ...).Wait()` patterns in tests; calls inside `ConfigureAwait(false)`-marked code paths where SC capture is explicitly suppressed; sites already surfaced by `async-correctness` rg battery — do NOT duplicate-flag. Cited from `async-await-misuse` split (was sub-clause a). |  | HIGH |
| async-void-not-event-handler | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `async void` method declaration in the diff: verify the signature matches a real event-handler shape (`void Foo(object? sender, EventArgs e)`, Blazor `void OnX(SomeEventArgs e)`, MAUI `Page.OnAppearing()` override, WinForms designer-generated handler). If it does NOT match an event-handler shape, the `async void` is wrong: exceptions in the body become unobservable AppDomain-crashing exceptions instead of awaited failures. **Fix**: change return type to `async Task` and have callers await it. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: actual event handlers (matching the framework signatures above); `async void Dispose()` overrides where the framework dictates the signature; sites already surfaced by `async-correctness` rg battery — do NOT duplicate-flag. Cited from `async-await-misuse` split (was sub-clause b). |  | HIGH |
| fire-and-forget-discard-unobserved | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `_ = SomeAsyncCall(...)` / discard-assigned `async Task` invocation in the diff: verify the discarded Task's exceptions are observed by either (a) explicit `.ContinueWith(t => Log(t.Exception), TaskContinuationOptions.OnlyOnFaulted)` chain, OR (b) a global `TaskScheduler.UnobservedTaskException` handler that logs/reports the failure, OR (c) explicit documentation that the call is best-effort and exceptions are acceptable. **High-signal failure**: `_ = _eventService.PublishAsync(evt);` where PublishAsync can throw `TimeoutException` → silent failure. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: discard with explicit `.ContinueWith(...OnlyOnFaulted)`; sites where the upstream Task is guaranteed non-faulting by contract (rare; cite the contract); sites already surfaced by `async-correctness` rg battery — do NOT duplicate-flag. Cited from `async-await-misuse` split (was sub-clause c). |  | HIGH |
| task-completedtask-from-async-named-method | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW method in the diff whose declaration returns `Task` / `Task<T>` / `ValueTask` / `ValueTask<T>` AND whose body returns `Task.CompletedTask` / `Task.FromResult(...)` / `ValueTask.CompletedTask` WITHOUT using `await`: verify the method's NAME and XML doc do NOT promise actual async work (verbs like "Reload", "Save", "Send", "Connect", "Refresh" that imply IO or background processing). If name promises async but body is synchronous, the signature is a LIE — callers will assume their `await methodAsync()` actually performs the work, but the work happens synchronously on the caller's thread (or, worse, doesn't happen at all). **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-8 round 10)**: `public Task ReloadAllActiveLogsAsync(...) { Dispatch(closeAction); foreach (...) OpenLog(...); return Task.CompletedTask; }` — returns instantly, dispatches fire-and-forget; caller's `await Reload...()` returns before any reload happens. Fix options: (a) make the body genuinely async (add `await` for each step); (b) rename the method to a non-async-implying verb (`EnqueueReload`, `RequestReload`); (c) document in the XML doc that it's a fire-and-forget dispatcher (and verify that's the intended contract). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: sync bridge/adapter implementations of an async-shaped interface contract where the contract documents the sync optimization (e.g., `public Task SaveAsync() { /* in-memory only */ return Task.CompletedTask; }` on an `IRepository` whose XML doc states sync semantics for the in-memory variant); test doubles / stubs / mocks that don't need async behavior; methods whose names are intrinsically completion-shaped (`Initialize`, `Configure`) where the caller doesn't expect IO. Distinct from `prefer-async-suffix` which targets sync calls INSIDE async methods; this rule targets async-promising signatures with sync bodies. Cited from `async-await-misuse` split (was sub-clause d). |  | HIGH |
| partial-cleanup-on-async-failure-path | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW method in the diff that (a) registers N resources / completion tokens / cancellation registrations / shared-state entries BEFORE entering an `await` loop, AND (b) the await loop can throw mid-iteration: verify the failure path cleans up ALL N registered entries (not just the entry whose await threw) AND attempts best-effort restoration of observable state for callers (e.g., reopen the snapshotted resources even when close phase failed midway — leaving the system in "all closed, none reopened" is worse than "best-effort reopened, throw still propagates"). **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-8 rounds 11+12)**: round 11 — `foreach (var (id, name, task) in waiters) { try { await task.WaitAsync(timeout, ct); } catch (...) { _coordinator.RemoveStrandedCompletion(id); throw; } }` — only the failed iteration's `id` is cleaned up; waiters for not-yet-awaited logs leak in `_logCloseCompletions`. Round 12 — even with the cleanup fixed, `ReloadAllActiveLogsAsync` threw immediately on close failure, leaving the UI's active-logs in the closed state with no reopen attempt. **Required fixes**: (1) catch loops over ALL registered entries (not just current iteration); (2) defer the throw via a local `Exception?` variable and attempt best-effort restoration (reopen / re-register / rollback) of the snapshotted pre-failure state BEFORE rethrowing. Pattern: `Exception? deferredFailure = null; try { ... } catch (...) { /* cleanup */; deferredFailure = ex; break; } /* outside try: best-effort restore */; if (deferredFailure is not null) { throw deferredFailure; }`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: registrations using `using` / `await using` declarations (compiler-generated cleanup is total); methods whose contract explicitly documents "fail-fast with no rollback" semantics; methods where the snapshot is already exposed to the caller and the caller handles restoration (e.g., `PrepareForDatabaseRemovalAsync` populates a caller-owned `LogReopenSnapshot` — caller invokes `ReopenAfterDatabaseRemoval` separately). **Audit method**: search NEW code for `RegisterX` / `Subscribe` / `Acquire` / `Allocate` outside a using/finally + inside a foreach + with await inside try/catch; for each occurrence, verify (a) catch loops over the registration set, (b) failure path attempts best-effort restoration of caller-observable state if no caller-snapshot mechanism exists. |  | HIGH |
| silent-cancellation-via-early-return-vs-throw | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `if (cancellationToken.IsCancellationRequested) { return; }` pattern in the diff inside a method returning `Task` / `Task<T>` / `ValueTask` / `ValueTask<T>`: verify the caller can distinguish "completed" from "cancelled" from the return alone. If the method silently returns a default value (or no value) when cancellation is requested mid-execution, callers awaiting the method see "complete" and proceed — even though only a prefix of the work actually ran. **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-8 round 11)**: `public async Task ApplyPendingTogglesAsync(IReadOnlyCollection<string> fileNames, CT ct) { foreach (var f in fileNames) { if (ct.IsCancellationRequested) { return; } await Toggle(f); } }` — if ct cancels after 2 of 5 toggles, the method returns void; caller assumes all 5 toggles ran. **Required fix**: use `cancellationToken.ThrowIfCancellationRequested()` so OperationCanceledException propagates and callers can distinguish completion from cancellation. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: methods where partial-completion semantics are explicitly documented in XML (`<remarks>Returns silently on cancellation; caller should re-check state</remarks>`); methods that signal partial completion via return value (`Task<int>` returning count of items processed); fire-and-forget paths whose callers don't await; loops that idempotently re-run on next invocation. **Audit method**: regex-grep `if\\s*\\(\\s*\\w+\\.IsCancellationRequested\\s*\\)\\s*\\{\\s*return\\s*;` in NEW diff hunks; for each match, check the enclosing method's signature + XML doc for explicit partial-completion semantics. |  | HIGH |
| await-throwable-async-call-without-handler-in-teardown-flow | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `await <async-method-that-can-throw>` in the diff inside a tear-down / dispose / save-and-close / modal-close / `OnDeactivating` / `OnBeforeUnload` / `IAsyncDisposable.DisposeAsync` / equivalent close-flow handler: verify exceptions are caught and either (a) routed to a banner/log surface, or (b) explicitly swallowed for known-transient cases (cancellation, timeout) WITH at minimum a `ITraceLogger.Warning` / `_logger.Warning($"... {ex}")` call so the failure is not invisible. An unhandled throw mid-teardown breaks the close flow and leaves UI / state in an inconsistent state (modal half-closed, partial state cleared, focus lost); a SILENT swallow without logging makes timeouts / transient failures invisible to operators. **High-signal failures**: round 11 — `if (yes) { await LogReloadCoordinator.ReloadAllActiveLogsAsync(); }` inside a modal's `PromptAndReloadOpenLogs` close-handler — `ReloadAllActiveLogsAsync` can throw `TimeoutException` / `OperationCanceledException` (per its documented contract); unhandled, the close flow breaks. Round 12 — even after wrapping in `try/catch (OCE or TimeoutException)`, the silent swallow loses the timeout signal; operators investigating "Settings closed but my open logs are stuck" have no diagnostic trail. **Required fix**: (a) for OCE swallow is acceptable (cancellation is expected during teardown — caller initiated cancel); (b) for TimeoutException MUST log via injected `ITraceLogger` (or equivalent) at Warning level with `{ex}` (full exception); (c) for unexpected exception types, route to error banner. Pattern: `try { await ... } catch (OperationCanceledException) { } catch (TimeoutException ex) { TraceLogger.Warning($"... reload did not complete within timeout: {ex}"); }`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: awaits where the caller-side already logs (look for `_logger.Trace` / `_logger.Warning` in the awaited method's own catch); awaits at the END of the close handler where any throw would propagate as `async void`-unobservable but the framework's exception filter catches it (rare — verify the framework actually has such a filter); awaits whose target method is documented as never-throwing. **Audit method**: identify NEW `await` calls inside methods named `Prompt*`, `OnClose*`, `OnDeactivat*`, `OnDispose*`, `Dispose*Async`, `Cleanup*`, `Teardown*`, `Save*AndClose*`. For each, verify the enclosing try/catch covers the call's documented exception set AND emits at least one log line per non-OCE swallow. |  | HIGH |
| log-exception-via-message-loses-stack-and-inner-context | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `_logger.Warning` / `_logger.Error` / `TraceLogger.Warning` / `TraceLogger.Error` / equivalent diagnostic log call in the diff that interpolates `{ex.Message}` (or `ex.Message` via concat): replace with `{ex}` to capture full stack trace + inner exception chain + AggregateException unwrapping. Diagnostic logs are for OPERATORS investigating field failures — losing the stack means losing the actual fault line; losing the InnerException means losing the actual cause (e.g., a `TaskCanceledException` wrapping the real `TimeoutException`). **High-signal failure (cited from consuming-pr-8 round 12)**: `_logger.Warning($"{operationName} failed: {ex.Message}");` — operator sees "import failed: Object reference not set to an instance of an object" with no clue where. With `{ex}`, the full stack appears: `at SomeService.LoadAsync()` etc. **Distinct from `_errorBanners.ReportError($"... {ex.Message}")`**: user-facing banner messages SHOULD use `ex.Message` (banners display in UI; stack traces would be unhelpful + leak internals). Distinction lives in WHERE the message goes: diagnostic logger sink → `{ex}`; user-facing UI surface → `{ex.Message}`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: log sites that ALREADY use `{ex}` (full); log sites where the surrounding context already provides `Exception.Data` / structured logging properties that capture stack separately; banners / toast messages / push notifications — those are user-facing UI surfaces, not operator diagnostics. **Audit method**: regex-grep `_logger\\.(Warning\|Error)\\(.*ex\\.Message\|TraceLogger\\.(Warning\|Error)\\(.*ex\\.Message`; for each match, confirm the sink is operator-diagnostic (not user-facing) and replace `ex.Message` with `ex`. |  | HIGH |
| magic-string-numeric-literal | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW string or numeric literal added in the diff appearing in a non-test code path that controls program behavior (compared in a switch, passed as a config key, used as a route segment, used as a timeout, used as a buffer size, used as a retry count, used as a port number, used as a magic byte sentinel): verify the literal is either (a) declared as a named constant (`const`/`readonly static`) with intent-documenting name, (b) sourced from configuration with a documented default, or (c) genuinely so small/simple it would be more confusing as a constant (0, 1, -1, "", `string.Empty`). **High-signal failure**: `Task.Delay(3000)` — what does 3000 represent? Should be `Task.Delay(StartupSettleDelayMilliseconds)` or `Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(3))`. Similarly: `if (status == "InProgress")` should be `if (status == StatusValues.InProgress)`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: literals in tests (test data, expected values, parametrized cases); literals in resource-key constants files; mathematical literals (0, 1, -1, π, e, 100 for percent); error message format strings ("Failed to open {0}"); literals that ARE the value being defined (i.e., the constant declaration site itself). Use the diff to find NEW behavior-controlling literals; pre-existing magic values in baseline are not in scope. |  |

## Patterns (lower-frequency, 3-4 hits in seed corpus)

| slug | scope_mode | params | review_pass_only_prompt | fp_slug |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| js-interop-lifecycle | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"JSRuntime\\.InvokeAsync<IJSObjectReference>\|catch \\(JSException\|catch \\(JSDisconnectedException","glob":["*.cs","*.razor.cs"]} |  |  |
| internals-visibility | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"InternalsVisibleTo","glob":["*.cs"]} |  |  |
| image-link-broken | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"!\\[.*\\]\\(","glob":["*.md"]} |  |  |
| partial-file-cleanup | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"new\\s+(ProviderDbContext\|FileStream\|StreamWriter).*new","glob":["*.cs"]} |  |  |
| perf-batching | review-pass-only | {} | For every operation/component in the diff that emits per-row, per-entry, or per-iteration UI updates (`StateHasChanged`, `Progress<T>.Report`, `InvokeAsync(StateHasChanged)`, event-handler chains): verify the update is batched/throttled — not called on every loop iteration that may run thousands of times. For every data-source enumeration (provider list, log entries, EF Core query results): verify single-pass — flag patterns like "load names first, then load details in a second pass" that imply N+1 or double-scan. For every async-foreach loop: verify the loop body doesn't await per-item if the workload is independent (consider `Task.WhenAll` + batching). |  |
| logic-inversion | review-pass-only | {} | For every early-return / short-circuit guard in modified `.cs`/`.razor.cs` files with predicates involving `!=`, `!`, `!=!`, or double-negatives: re-read the guard and verify it exits when state is the desired state, not when state is the OPPOSITE. Common bug: `if (_show != !isPinned) return;` — when `_show == !isPinned` (desired-state-already-set), this DOES exit; otherwise it doesn't. Verify the polarity matches intent. Ternary expressions returning the wrong branch (`condition ? badValue : goodValue`) and cross-method state flips where state is inverted twice and end up wrong are also caught here. |  |
| iasyncdisposable-fp | review-pass-only | {} | For every `await using` over a BCL type: verify the type implements `IAsyncDisposable` per Microsoft docs. Common FP: `ServiceProvider` (does implement both since .NET Core 3.0); `Stream` (does since .NET Core 3.0). Confirmed IDisposable-only: `SemaphoreSlim`, `HttpClient` (per .NET 8 docs). Cross-reference fp-2 below. | fp-2 |
| off-by-one-bounds | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW loop / index expression / range slice / `<= length` vs `< length` comparison / `array[length - N]` indexing / `Span.Slice(start, length)` added in the diff: verify the bounds are correct against the underlying collection size. **High-signal failures**: `for (int i = 0; i <= array.Length; i++)` — off by one (overruns); `array[array.Length - 1]` on an empty array — out of bounds; `text.Substring(start, length)` where `start + length > text.Length`; `Span.Slice(offset, count)` where `offset + count` overflows the span; pagination math `skip = (page - 1) * pageSize` with `page = 0`; `list.RemoveAt(0)` in a tight loop without re-checking `list.Count`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: bounds where the loop-invariant proves correctness AND the proof is documented in a one-line comment; pre-checked emptiness (`if (array.Length == 0) return; ... array[0]`); BCL methods that are documented to throw rather than corrupt (`ImmutableArray.Builder.Add` cannot overflow). Use the diff to find NEW bounds-sensitive operations; pre-existing patterns are not in scope. |  |
| input-validation-missing | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW public method / public API endpoint / `[Parameter]` setter / `[HttpPost]` / `[HttpPut]` handler / event handler that takes untrusted input (`string`, byte[], collection, deserialized DTO) added in the diff: verify the input is validated at the entry point against length, format, range, allowed-value set, or other domain invariants. **High-signal failures**: HTTP endpoint accepts `string note` with no `[StringLength]` / `[MaxLength]` — DoS via gigabyte payload; method takes `int count` without `count > 0` check, leading to negative-loop math; takes `DateTime when` with no validation, allowing `DateTime.MinValue` propagation; takes `string filePath` consumed via `File.ReadAllBytes` with no validation. **Required defense**: `ArgumentException.ThrowIfNullOrEmpty(name)`, `ArgumentOutOfRangeException.ThrowIfNegativeOrZero(count)`, `[Required] [StringLength(...)]` attributes, custom domain validator at the entry point. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: internal methods consumed only by validated callers (trace the lineage); strongly-typed-id wrapper types whose constructor already validated (cross-reference `strongly-typed-id-preference`); inputs already validated by middleware (FluentValidation pipeline, model-binding [Required]). Use the diff to find NEW entry points; pre-existing unvalidated APIs in baseline are not in scope. |  |
| xss-injection-risk | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW user-controlled value rendered into HTML / JavaScript / SQL / shell context added in the diff: verify proper encoding/escaping/parameterization. **High-signal failures**: Razor `@((MarkupString)userInput)` — bypasses Blazor\u0027s default HTML encoding; `string sql = $"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = \u0027{name}\u0027";` — SQL injection via concatenation; `Process.Start("cmd.exe", $"/c echo {userInput}")` — command injection; `Response.WriteAsync($"<div>{userMessage}</div>")` — XSS via direct write without `HtmlEncoder`. **Required defenses**: `HtmlEncoder.Default.Encode(value)`, parameterized SQL (`SqlCommand.Parameters.Add`), shell-argument escaping (`Process.Start(new ProcessStartInfo { FileName = "cmd.exe", ArgumentList = { ... } })`), Blazor\u0027s default `@expression` encoding (do NOT use `@((MarkupString)...)` unless the source is already encoded). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: values from compile-time constants; values from an already-validated allow-list; values rendered into a context that explicitly tolerates the format (`<script>` JSON-stringified via `JsonSerializer.Serialize` — Blazor already handles); values run through a documented sanitization library. Use the diff to find NEW user-controlled render sites; pre-existing patterns in baseline are not in scope. |  |
| shell-script-discipline | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"^#!/(usr/)?bin/(ba)?sh","glob":["**/*.sh","**/.devcontainer/setup*"]} |  |  |
| code-duplication-dry | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW block of code added in the diff (>=5 lines): scan the diff and the surrounding files (within the same project / src tree) for substantively-identical blocks. **High-signal patterns**: same parameter assignment repeated across N method overloads (`SetA(...) { /* boilerplate */ Foo(x); } SetB(...) { /* same boilerplate */ Foo(y); }` — factor out the boilerplate); same try/catch/log block repeated across three different services; **same try/catch/exception-routing shape repeated across N≥2 methods OF THE SAME NEW FILE** (e.g., `DispatchSafelyAsync(Action)` and `OpenRecoveryDialogAsync(Func<Task>)` both wrap an outer `try { await dispatch(() => { try { work } catch { route } }); } catch { route }` — extract a shared inner-catch helper `SafeInvoke(Action)` / `SafeInvokeAsync(Func<Task>)` so the outer dispatch only owns the dispatch-level catch); same validation-then-throw sequence in three constructors; same DTO mapping repeated across read/write methods. Flag the duplication and propose: (a) shared private helper, (b) base-class template method, (c) generic parameterization, (d) source-generator if the pattern is highly mechanical. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: duplication that aids isolation (e.g., test fixtures intentionally repeated to avoid cross-test coupling); duplication that\u0027s likely to diverge soon (single point of variation pending); duplication across security-boundary or assembly-boundary where coupling them would be worse; 5-line "duplications" that are actually just identical boilerplate signatures (`public override void Dispose() => ...`) without shared logic. **REPEAT-MISS DISCIPLINE (2x in lightweight-gate-v5 history — PR 4 `TryOpenModalAsync` helper + PR 5 `OpenRecoveryDialogAsync`)**: when a NEW file contains N≥2 methods with try/catch shapes, panel slots MUST explicitly enumerate them by name and compare shapes before declaring READY. Cross-reference: §2C `DRY remediation gate` covers commit-time DRY audit; this rule covers in-PR-review-time DRY observation. Use the diff to find NEW blocks; baseline duplications are out of scope (separate refactor work). |  |

---

## FP-1: parent-namespace-not-in-scope

**Technical claim**: a child namespace requires an explicit `using` directive to reference an identifier declared in its parent namespace.

**Why FP**: Per C# spec ECMA-334 § 13.8 (Namespace declarations) / C# 7 spec § 3.8 (Namespace and type names), simple-name lookup walks enclosing namespaces from innermost to outermost. A type declared in a parent namespace is visible from any descendant namespace without a `using` directive. Build green = lookup succeeded = claim is wrong.

**Recurrence pattern**: tends to recur across multiple PRs touching the same parent/child namespace pair; Copilot's pattern matcher doesn't apply the C# language rule consistently. Phrasing varies ("isn't in scope", "won't compile", "requires explicit using") — match on technical claim, not exact wording.

**Canonical dismissal template**:
> False positive — C# child namespaces have implicit access to parent-namespace identifiers per ECMA-334 § 13.8 / C# 7 spec § 3.8. The file's namespace is a descendant of the target type's namespace; no `using` directive is required. Build green at the cited commit confirms the lookup succeeds. Recurring FP per `pattern-catalog.md` FP-1.

**Mitigation candidates** (deferred for low FP cost): add explicit `using <parent-namespace>;` per file (1 line per file; reduces FP but pollutes the using list); move type into descendant namespace if domain semantics permit (forces a namespace where it doesn't semantically belong). Status: project-by-project decision.

## FP-2: await-using-on-idisposable-only-type

**Technical claim**: the type returned at the use site implements `IDisposable` but NOT `IAsyncDisposable`, so `await using` will not compile.

**Why FP**: agent must verify the actual type at the use site. Specifically:
- `Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.ServiceProvider` — implements BOTH `IDisposable` AND `IAsyncDisposable` since .NET Core 3.0 (2019). See [ServiceProvider class reference](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.dependencyinjection.serviceprovider).
- `System.IO.Stream` — implements BOTH since .NET Core 3.0. See [Stream.DisposeAsync](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.io.stream.disposeasync).
- `System.Threading.SemaphoreSlim` and `System.Net.Http.HttpClient` are `IDisposable`-only as of .NET 8; do NOT add them to the dismissal list.

Build evidence: if `await using var x = ...` compiles, the type implements `IAsyncDisposable`. Period.

**Recurrence pattern**: Copilot's pattern matcher appears to have stale framework knowledge about which BCL types implement `IAsyncDisposable`. Tends to recur in clusters (all N CLI command files in a single review batch).

**Canonical dismissal template**:
> False positive — `<TypeName>` implements both `IDisposable` AND `IAsyncDisposable` since `<framework-version>` (cite: `<docs-url>`). Project targets `<consuming-project-tfm>`. `await using` compiles correctly. Build green at the cited commit confirms. Recurring FP per `pattern-catalog.md` FP-2.

**Mitigation candidates**: none worth pursuing. Fix is on Copilot's side. Optional: explicit type annotation at the use site (`await using ServiceProvider sp = ...`) makes the interface implementation more obvious to Copilot's matcher — may reduce FP recurrence.

## FP-3: readonly-field-cannot-be-out-parameter-in-ctor

**Technical claim**: passing a `readonly` instance field as an `out` parameter is illegal C# / will not compile.

**Why FP**: C# language rule (ECMA-334 §15.5.3 / Roslyn `CheckIsValidReceiverForVariableOrError`): `readonly` fields are writable inside the declaring class's instance constructor (and the declaring struct's instance constructor) — they are in a *definite-assignment* context identical to direct assignment, so they can be passed as `out`/`ref` arguments from constructor body. The restriction (`CS0192` / `CS0199`) applies OUTSIDE the constructor. Pattern in question:

```csharp
public sealed class Foo
{
    private readonly IService _service;

    public Foo()
    {
        // LEGAL — assignment via out happens inside the declaring class's ctor.
        SomeHelper.Provide(out _service);
    }
}
```

Build evidence: if `private readonly X _field; public Foo() { Bar(out _field); }` compiles, the language rule was satisfied. Period.

**Recurrence pattern**: Copilot's reviewer matches on "readonly" + "out" without distinguishing ctor scope from method scope. The same FP triggers any time a test fixture uses a helper like `AddBannerSubstitutes(out var ...)` to initialize multiple readonly fields in one call.

**Canonical dismissal template**:
> False positive — C# permits passing readonly fields as `out` parameters inside the declaring class's instance constructor (definite-assignment context per ECMA-334 §15.5.3). The restriction (`CS0192`/`CS0199`) applies only outside the constructor. Build green + tests pass at the cited commit confirm the code compiles. Recurring FP per `pattern-catalog.md` FP-3.

**Mitigation candidates**: none necessary; the pattern is idiomatic for test fixtures that use a helper method to construct + assign multiple substitutes in one call. Optional: a `(Foo, Bar, Baz) tuple` return shape on the helper would sidestep the FP at the cost of two extra lines per fixture and worse named-out-param discoverability — typically not worth it.

---

## Catalog maintenance

After each PR final state (converged-to-zero OR merged OR closed-with-deferred-findings-tracker):

1. Append findings to the global `data/findings.csv` (per its schema).
2. Re-run the regex classifier against the corpus. If a NEW pattern appears with frequency ≥3 across the corpus, propose adding to this catalog (PR to CopilotInstructions) with abstract phrasing.
3. If a pattern's hit rate is decaying (fewer hits round-over-round), demote it from high-frequency battery to lower-frequency section. Demotions require ≥2 consecutive rounds of zero-hits to confirm decay.
4. Update FP entries if Copilot raises a new FP worth deterring (≥2 recurrences + source-grounded dismissal).
5. Commit catalog updates to `CopilotInstructions/<branch>`. Catalog is project-deidentified; no per-project data ever lands here.
